paragraphs.text: UFC-Que Choisir

UFC-Que Choisir has a network of 129 local associations across the country, which supports consumers in resolving 100 000 disputes every year. Its 130 employees, based in Paris, publish articles and surveys for the magazine and the website 'Que Choisir', conduct comparative tests, initiate legal actions, and carry out economic studies and advocacy to inform and work on decisions to make consumption more efficient and responsible for everyone.
www.quechoisir.org
Publisher: UFC-Que Choisir

This document is subject to copyright but can be used freely for campaign, education and research purposes provided the source is fully acknowledged. For copying in any other circumstances, reuse in other publications, translation or adaptation, permission must be granted and fees may be requested.
Contact: fmichaelsen@quechoisir.org

evidence_quotes.quote: unfair practices aimed at influencing individuals to take decisions that are contrary to their interests.

paragraphs.text: At a time when the Internet has established itself as an integral part of our quoti dienne life, consumers and consommatrices are increasingly confronted with situations which they legitimately perceive as unfair or even abusive. While digital progress has brought huge benefits in terms of access to goods and services, it has also risked the proliferation of unfair practices aimed at influencing individuals to take decisions that are contrary to their interests.

evidence_quotes.quote: Dark patterns manipulating user choices
customisation of offers based on increasingly intrusive data collection
price manipulation

paragraphs.text: There are many examples that are familiar to everyone. Dark patterns manipulating user choices, customisation of offers based on increasingly intrusive data collection, price manipulation or addictive design: these practices, now pervasive in the digital space, are causing considerable damage. According to the European Commission, they represent a collective annual loss of EUR 7.9 billion for consumers and consumers in Europe.

evidence_quotes.quote: The listeningui evolves in an environment where every click can be observed, each orientated choice, every hesitation exploited.

paragraphs.text: But their effects are not limited to economic losses alone. They are key to individual autonomy, privacy and psychological well-being. The listeningui evolves in an environment where every click can be observed, each orientated choice, every hesitation exploited. This constant and insidious pressure leads to cognitive overload, loss of confidence and ultimately a deterioration in mental health. The consequences are also visible at the level of society: the overcrowding encouraged by these mechanisms harms the environment, weakens fair competition and jeopardises market transparency.

In the face of these abuses, the current regulatory framework shows its critical limitations. There is an urgent need to adapt the rules to the realities of the 21st century to ensure that technological progress remains at the service of, and not at the expense of, citizens. This is where the UFC-Que Choisir puts forward these proposals for a future Digital Fairness Act (DFA), designed to create a fairer, more transparent and truly consumer-friendly digital environment.
Marie-Amandine STÉVENIN President of UFC-Que Choisir
The Digital Fairness Act: UFC-Que positions choose | 5

For several years, UFC-Que Choisir has been warning about the proliferation of unfair commercial practices in the digital space that weaken consumer protection. The Digital Fairness Fitness Check, an in-depth assessment carried out by the European Commission, has confirmed these findings indisputably. This analysis revealed a twofold worrying reality: on the one hand, the effective application of existing rules and, on the other hand, the need for a substantial strengthening of the legal framework to deal with new forms of imbalance in the digital economy.

evidence_quotes.quote: the use of dark patterns manipulating user behaviour
the insertion of unfair terms in the terms and conditions
the display of misleading prices
the promotion of virtual currencies.

paragraphs.text: First of all, many practices observed today are already prohibited by European consumer law, but are still being used on a large scale, with impunity. There are many examples: the use of dark patterns manipulating user behaviour, the insertion of unfair terms in the terms and conditions, the display of misleading prices or the promotion of virtual currencies.

The continued existence of such practices is due in particular to a legal framework based on general but imprecise principles. This approach has the advantage of being flexible and able to apply to a wide variety of situations, including the emergence of new technologies and new business models. However, this flexibility has a cost: the application of broad provisions depends on case-by-case interpretation, and their implementation becomes complex and slow.

However, the European Commission has issued precise guidance on the interpretation of these rules, covering almost all of the problematic practices identified. But as the Fitness Check reveals, these guidelines are not sufficient to ensure respect for the law.

For this reason, several measures advocated in this note do not involve creating new obligations, but seek to clarify explicitly in the law that certain practices, which are already prohibited in theory, are indeed prohibited in practice. Such clarification would increase legal certainty, simplify enforcement for businesses and authorities, and thus contribute to better consumer protection.

evidence_quotes.quote: addictive design
new forms of influence marketing

paragraphs.text: Second, in addition to these necessary clarifications, some emerging practices require the introduction of new substantive rules. To reduce the future Digital Fairness Act (DFA) to a simplification of existing law would ignore market realities. Phenomena such as addictive design or new forms of influence marketing are still largely outside the current legal framework and require targeted and ambitious responses.

evidence_quotes.quote: privileged access to data and almost full control over the interface, thus over the interaction itself with consumers.

paragraphs.text: Finally, the success of the EPR will depend primarily on its effective application. Strengthening substantive law will not be enough: procedural rules will also need to be adapted to take account of digital asymmetry, i.e. structural imbalance between consumers and traders. In the digital environment, businesses have privileged access to data and almost full control over the interface, thus over the interaction itself with consumers. In this situation, certain adjustments are necessary, such as reversing the burden of proof where it is materially impossible for the consumer to demonstrate that a practice is unfair.

This note sets out the positions and recommendations of UFC-Que Choose on the future Digital Fairness Act (DFA), developed over recent years from our own studies and expressed through our European Federation, the European Consumer Organisation (BEUC). These positions, shared by the European consumerist movement as a whole, are an essential contribution to building a fairer, more transparent and truly consumer-friendly digital environment.
F C U C
The Digital Fairness Act: UFC-Que positions choose | 6

evidence_quotes.quote: Dark patterns, or deceptive patterns, are manipulative techniques used by e-commerce companies to influence consumer deception.

paragraphs.text: Dark patterns, or deceptive patterns, are manipulative techniques used by e-commerce companies to influence consumer deception. They exploit their cognitive biases in order to induce them to take disappointment that they would not otherwise have taken.

evidence_quotes.quote: repeated inducements, such as opening windows or typing or punitive clips, which direct the consumer to a specific action.
Others are based on stock or time counters, which are supposed to indicate that a promotion is coming to an end soon or that there is only one discarticle left, thus pushing for impulsive purchase.

paragraphs.text: These practices take a variety of forms. Among the most common are repeated inducements, such as opening windows or typing or punitive clips, which direct the consumer to a specific action. Others are based on stock or time counters, which are supposed to indicate that a promotion is coming to an end soon or that there is only one discarticle left, thus pushing for impulsive purchase. Similar strategic patented names exist, such as making it more visible or easier to choose the most advantageous option for the platform.

section_path: most marketplaces crowded use dark patterns

evidence_quotes.quote: The effects of dark patterns are multiple and preoccupants.

paragraphs.text: The effects of dark patterns are multiple and preoccupants. They translate into economic, environmental andgic psycholocconsequences for consumers. From an economic point of view, they can lead to unwanted expenses and financial losses for the consumer, which is incentivised to buy more than it would otherwise have done. From an environmental point of view, this stimulates overconsumption, which is a brake on responsible consumption and thus a negative environmental impact. Finally, psychologically, they have a sense of urgency, guilt or frustration, which can contribute to a feeling of unease on the part of the consumer.

evidence_quotes.quote: video games offer a particularly illustrative example.

paragraphs.text: Today, on average, 7 out of 10 French people play video games, ranging from younger to old. However, unfortunately, this sectoris emblematic of many unfair-mercial practices affecting a wide audience. Although these practices are not limited to the moot of the video game and their management must extend to all digital services (including social networks, e-commerce sites or streaming services), video games offer a particularly illustrative example.

evidence_quotes.quote: loot boxes, these pay-gaming mechanisms incorporated into video games and enabling the release of new content.

paragraphs.text: Since 2017, the UFC-Que Choisir alert on loot boxes, these pay-gaming mechanisms incorporated into video games and enabling the release of new content. They are virtual objects, often depicted in the form of safes-containing allegedly randomly assigned items. These elements may be purecosmetics (such as a different garment for a character) or essential for progression in the game (more powerful weapons, super-plumbing-vehicles, etc.). Loot boxes can sometimes be obtained free of charge during the game, but they are most often accessible against actual payment by bank card.

evidence_quotes.quote: make it very slow without purchase
to pay for quick progress

paragraphs.text: When the objects obtained directly influence progression, developers often calibrate the game so as to make it very slow without purchase. The player is then confronted with a biased choice: to pay for quick progress or long hours to play, at the risk of being put at a disadvantage vis-à-vis other players who have spent real money.

evidence_quotes.quote: in multi-player games

paragraphs.text: Cosmetic objects can reach considerable values, be it a symbolic value of social status in multi-player games or a real market value. Some of these virtual cosmetic objects could even besold for tens of thousands of euros.

evidence_quotes.quote: loot boxes

paragraphs.text: The system for releasing content inherent in the loot boxes involves, from the consumer's point of view, randomly within the game: the player pays for an object, without knowing which in advance, and the winnings are determined only after payment. Although
the award is systematic, it is unknown, whether or not it is uncertain, depending on the practice actually adopted by the trader.

evidence_quotes.quote: This logic is reinforced by an audiovisual presentation inspired by slot machines: attractive sounds, flashing lights, dyna visual effects and latency prior to the revelation of the gain, all of which are intended to stimulate excitation and impulsive dice.

This logic is reinforced by an audiovisual presentation inspired by slot machines: attractive sounds, flashing lights, dyna visual effects and latency prior to the revelation of the gain, all of which are intended to stimulate excitation and impulsive dice.

paragraphs.text: This logic is reinforced by an audiovisual presentation inspired by slot machines: attractive sounds, flashing lights, dyna visual effects and latency prior to the revelation of the gain, all of which are intended to stimulate excitation and impulsive dice.

evidence_quotes.quote: those loot boxes present the teristic carac of lotteries
assimilated to games of chance

paragraphs.text: In the light of Article L. 322-2 of the Internal Security Code, those loot boxes present the teristic carac of lotteries and, as such, should be strictly regulated. It is unacceptable that a often young audience is exposed to these nismsthat can be assimilated to games of chance, the addictive effects and financial consequences of which are well documented.

evidence_quotes.quote: Classifying loot boxes as games of chance would therefore be consistent to ensure an equivalent level of protection, in particular for minors.

paragraphs.text: Classifying loot boxes as games of chance would therefore be consistent to ensure an equivalent level of protection, in particular for minors. However, such a measure remains difficult to apply in a European market where the regulation of gambling is a matter for the Member States.

evidence_quotes.quote: loot boxes in the video games

paragraphs.text: In the face of this regulatory deadlock, UFC-Que Choisir requests, in the context of the tax ruling, the outright interdic of loot boxes in the video games, in order to effectively protect consumers, in particular the younger ones, from these predatoryticks.
Our action on loot boxes: Winnings, vilain games

evidence_quotes.quote: More and more video games offer the purchase of virtualjets – such as weapons, costumes or tools – or additional functionalities that can be used in the game to improve the experience or progress faster.
It is impossible to buy such virtual objects or features with euro in many games.
Thus, before making a purchase, the consumer is obliged to convert his euro into the specific currency of the game.

paragraphs.text: More and more video games offer the purchase of virtualjets – such as weapons, costumes or tools – or additional functionalities that can be used in the game to improve the experience or progress faster. It is impossible to buy such virtual objects or features with euro in many games. My paid virtual raysin the form of dots, coins or diamonds should be used. Thus, before making a purchase, the consumer is obliged to convert his euro into the specific currency of the game.

evidence_quotes.quote: to buy these virtual currencies.

paragraphs.text: These virtual currencies are increasingly recurring. While 42% of the most played games on PCs use it, this rate reaches 81% in the most popular smartphone games. In France, 3.6 million players have already converted money into games to buy these virtual currencies.

evidence_quotes.quote: video game publishers are talking about their real cost and thus misleading consumers for a specific purpose: let them forget that the money they spend is real.
the exchange rate generally varies according to the formula chosen.

paragraphs.text: Through the use of such virtual currencies and in the presence of any display of the price in euros of the virtual objects offered, video game publishers are talking about their real cost and thus misleading consumers for a specific purpose: let them forget that the money they spend is real. This is all the more difficult because it is even impossible to calculate precisely how much virtual currency expenditure actually costs them in the end, as these currencies are only offered in pack and the exchange rate generally varies according to the formula chosen. However, it should be borne in mind that it is legal for consumers to know, in an intelligible and unequivocal manner, the price and the way in which it is calculated.

evidence_quotes.quote: donot correspond exactly to the 'prices' displayed for virtual objects.
Consumers are therefore obliged to buy and convert more money than necessary to obtain the desired object.

paragraphs.text: Furthermore, propo virtual currency packs often donot correspond exactly to the 'prices' displayed for virtual objects. Consumers are therefore obliged to buy and convert more money than necessary to obtain the desired object. Such practices significantly affect consumers' freedom of choice. Of course, the remaining amount of virtual currency will not be lost, but it will often be insufficient to buy any new items. The virtual currency portfolio will therefore have to be charged, thus creating a real vicious circle.
**81%** the most popular games use currencies misleading virtual

evidence_quotes.quote: virtual currencies are uniquely present in the world of video games

paragraphs.text: Moreover, while virtual currencies are uniquely present in the world of video games, they are now spread in other dry players, such as social networks or commerce platforms. Their misleading character has also been confirmed by the European Network of Consumer Protection Authorities (CPC Network) in response to a complaint lodged by UFC-Que Choisir and its European counterparts.

evidence_quotes.quote: virtual paid currencies

paragraphs.text: In response to these findings, our association calls for the prohibition in the EPR of all forms of virtual paid currencies in the naked services.

evidence_quotes.quote: banning policies aimed at sanctioning fraudulent behaviour.

paragraphs.text: In multi-player games, chewing some cipient parties can damage the experience of all players. To address this, most publishers have introduced banning policies aimed at sanctioning fraudulent behaviour. While the disappears to be legitimate, these measures give riseto a great deal of abuse.

evidence_quotes.quote: Many consumers complain that they have been excluded from gambling without reason or prior, sometimes temporary, permanent treatment.
the banner is not communicated

paragraphs.text: Many consumers complain that they have been excluded from gambling without reason or prior, sometimes temporary, permanent treatment. In many cases, the banner is not communicated, and players have no means of redress with the publisher. Where steps are possible, replies often rely on the use of cheating or cheating, without providing any evidence.

evidence_quotes.quote: invested hundreds of euros, particularly in my rays and virtual objects, to improve their playground.
these abusive bannings are akin to a form of premature obsolescence, as the game paid is no longer operable.

paragraphs.text: The final banning is all the more problematic given that some players have sometimes invested hundreds of euros, particularly in my rays and virtual objects, to improve their playground. This amount is obviously in addition to the purchase price of the game, which is already significant. It should be noted that some players are banned only one day after buying the game. Moreover, these abusive bannings are akin to a form of premature obsolescence, as the game paid is no longer operable.

evidence_quotes.quote: arbitrary banning of players

paragraphs.text: Indeed, UFC-Que Choisir did so in 2024 by lodging a complaint against Activision's publisher for arbitrary banning of players.

evidence_quotes.quote: abusive bannings
sums invested in virtual objects that have become inaccessible.

paragraphs.text: More broadly, we request that the ATD clarify in the Unfair Terms Directive the protection of consumers against abusive bannings, explicitly guaranteeing them the right to challenge their exclusion and, where appropriate, obtain reimbursement of sums invested in virtual objects that have become inaccessible.
Our action against Activision: Two complaints of abusive banning of players

evidence_quotes.quote: severely restrict users' rights to freely dispose of their purchases.

paragraphs.text: More and more video games, but also other cultural goods (music, books), are now purchased in dematerialised format. While this method of acquisition offers undeniable advantages for consumers, it is also used by publishers to severely restrict users' rights to freely dispose of their purchases.

evidence_quotes.quote: Thus, consumers cannot generally resell their dematerialised video games.
a publisher can fully withdraw access to a game that has been purchased, as well as to in-game purchases, in particular when closing the servers of a game requiring online authentication.

paragraphs.text: Thus, consumers cannot generally resell their dematerialised video games. Worse still, a publisher can fully withdraw access to a game that has been purchased, as well as to in-game purchases, in particular when closing the servers of a game requiring online authentication.

evidence_quotes.quote: Publishers claim that consumers acquire only a licence and not ownership of the game, even though transactions are presented as purchases.

paragraphs.text: Publishers claim that consumers acquire only a licence and not ownership of the game, even though transactions are presented as purchases. This logic sometimes even extends to games bought on a physical medium, which become unusable if the publisher decides to cease their technical support.

evidence_quotes.quote: to ensure the possibility of retaining and using the cultural goods purchased.

paragraphs.text: While a publisher cannot be required to maintain its services indefinitely, it should nevertheless be required to design its services in such a way as to ensure the possibility of retaining and using the cultural goods purchased.
In the light of those findings, UFC-Que Choisir requests that the tax ruling clarify and strengthen the rights of consumers in relation to tangible content, by providing, inter alia:
A guarantee enabling consumer to resell their digital games;

evidence_quotes.quote: A guarantee of sustainable access to their content, including: by the put to provision of an autonomous version if the service is stopped.

paragraphs.text: A guarantee of sustainable access to their content, including: by the put to provision of an autonomous version if the service is stopped.
Our action against Steam: Re-sale of dematerialised video games – the part may not be finished

evidence_quotes.quote: display of price reductions
the lowest price charged by the seller during the 30 days preceding the start of the promotion.

paragraphs.text: Since 2022, European Directive 2019/2161, known as the Modernisation Directive, has regulated the display of price reductions. It requires that any promotion be calculated on the basis of the lowest price charged by the seller during the 30 days preceding the start of the promotion.

evidence_quotes.quote: artificial prices
the illusion of large rebates

paragraphs.text: However, many professionals confine this obligation by using "comparator" prices. The latter serve as a bitrary reference to show an alleged reduction. By freely setting this benchmark price, sellers create the illusion of large rebates and thus induce consumers to buy by thinking of a good business. In the vast majority of cases, promotions displayed online are based on these artificial prices.
strikethrough prices are: false promotions

evidence_quotes.quote: Thus, where a strikethrough price is accompanied by a reference such as 'recommended selling price', 'origine', 'old price' or 'average price on competing sites', that is not a real reduction.

paragraphs.text: Thus, where a strikethrough price is accompanied by a reference such as 'recommended selling price', 'origine', 'old price' or 'average price on competing sites', that is not a real reduction.

evidence_quotes.quote: These "comparator" prices are not only pervasive but also difficult to understand and verify.
Some sellers simply do not provide any explanation as to the nature of the strikethrough price displayed.
And where an explanation is accessible, it is often based on intra-vendor data that is not verifiable by consumers.

paragraphs.text: These "comparator" prices are not only pervasive but also difficult to understand and verify. Some sellers simply do not provide any explanation as to the nature of the strikethrough price displayed. Others include their definitions in their general terms and conditions. And where an explanation is accessible, it is often based on intra-vendor data that is not verifiable by consumers.

evidence_quotes.quote: Thus, all strikethrough prices, regardless of their type, are displayed in a uniform manner (same size, same
presenting these comparison prices in the same visual format as genuine promotions.

paragraphs.text: This opacity is further reinforced by the common practice of presenting these comparison prices in the same visual format as genuine promotions. Thus, all strikethrough prices, regardless of their type, are displayed in a uniform manner (same size, same

evidence_quotes.quote: colour, same font), which makes it even more difficult for consumers to differentiate with a real price reduction.

paragraphs.text: colour, same font), which makes it even more difficult for consumers to differentiate with a real price reduction.

evidence_quotes.quote: misleading reduction

paragraphs.text: Consumers are therefore unable to easily conceive of a genuine reduction of a misleading reduction, and may be allowed to override the purchase of products that they believe in the balance.

evidence_quotes.quote: A study carried out in 2023 by UFC-Que Choisir on more than 6 000 announcements with a strikethrough priceshows that only 3.4% of these announcements respond to real promotions in line with the Modernisation Directive.

paragraphs.text: A study carried out in 2023 by UFC-Que Choisir on more than 6 000 announcements with a strikethrough priceshows that only 3.4% of these announcements respond to real promotions in line with the Modernisation Directive. Thus, 96.6% of thenon-compliances analysed are TROM practices, a finding confirmed by a second study carried out by our association in 2025.

evidence_quotes.quote: prohibition of any reference price system other than that provided for in the Modernisation Directive to be included in the tax ruling.

paragraphs.text: Faced with this situation, UFC-Que Choisir calls for the prohibition of any reference price system other than that provided for in the Modernisation Directive to be included in the tax ruling.

evidence_quotes.quote: Influence marketing has become a major issue in the protection of online summers in recent years.

paragraphs.text: Influence marketing has become a major issue in the protection of online summers in recent years. Influencers are now an incontemporary advertising resource for businesses. A significant part of the content posted on social media is, at least in part, advertisements that encourage internet users to buy various products or to be defective.

evidence_quotes.quote: especially when the commercial nature of a message is not clearly indicated.
influencers exploit their parasitic relationship with their subscribers, that is to say, the relationship of trust and proximity perceived by them, in order to influence their purchasing behaviour.

paragraphs.text: Poorly framed, this form of advertising presents a number of risks. On the one hand, the boundary between authentic and advertising is often blurred, especially when the commercial nature of a message is not clearly indicated. Second, influencers exploit their parasitic relationship with their subscribers, that is to say, the relationship of trust and proximity perceived by them, in order to influence their purchasing behaviour. This emotional dimension enhances the effectiveness of the messages that maybe blicitous, but also their potential for manipulation.

evidence_quotes.quote: regulates influence marketing.

paragraphs.text: In France, the 'influencers' law, which entered into force in 2023, regulates influence marketing. It points out that the general rules on advertising follow influence content and draws up a list of goods and services the promotion of which is prohibited or restricted, such as tobacco, certain technological products, games of chance and certain medical products. The law also requires influencers to explicitly state that content may be blicitous when it is.

evidence_quotes.quote: In addition, the rules should pre-empt the way in which advertising is to appear.
the term "commercial collaboration" allowed by the current legislation remains ambiguous and potentially misleading.

paragraphs.text: However, improvements are still lacking. UFC-Que Choisir calls, inter alia, for unhealthy food products to be added to the list of products banned for promotion to children. In addition, the rules should pre-empt the way in which advertising is to appear. Indeed, only the reference to "advertising" ga denotes clear and transparent information for the consumer, whereas the term "commercial collaboration" allowed by the current legislation remains ambiguous and potentially misleading.
Dissemination platforms also have a responsibility. They monitor the interface and the tips made available

evidence_quotes.quote: flag sponsored content.
clear and uniform disclosure of the advertising of content

paragraphs.text: to influencers to flag sponsored content. It is therefore essential to oblige them to offer technical functionalities enabling clear and uniform disclosure of the advertising of content, by defining in law the precise parameters (duration, size, neck, typography, etc.) of these terms. UFC-Que Choisir therefore pleads for joint responsibility between influencers, platforms, promoted brands and, where appropriate, agents of influence.

evidence_quotes.quote: influence marketing

paragraphs.text: The UFC-Que Choisir, while welcoming the progress of the Swedish legislation, calls for these achievements to be consolidated in future European rules on influence marketing.
Thus, UFC-Que Choisir requests that the following principles be taken into account in the tax ruling:
The current level of protection in France should not be weakened.
Existing rules on advertising should fully apply to influence marketing.
Transparency towards consumers must be ensured.
Responsibilities must be shared between the influencer, the platform, the brand and the agent of influence.
Our position on the 'influencers' law: After the smoke screen, the white smoke?
Our European position: From influto responsibility - Time to regulate influent marketing

evidence_quotes.quote: targeted online advertising is ubiquitous
Every day, consumers' personal data are tracked, sold and used without their knowledge.
the personal data collected can be shared more than 4 000 times, with more than 1 000 third parties.
activity relies on tracking and analysing the behaviour of summons for advertising purposes.

paragraphs.text: Today, targeted online advertising is ubiquitous. Every day, consumers' personal data are tracked, sold and used without their knowledge. The UFC-Que Choisir tests show that by consulting just around ten of the most popular sites in France, the personal data collected can be shared more than 4 000 times, with more than 1 000 third parties. These treasures, which are largely unknown to consumers, are online advertising providers and data brokers, whose activity relies on tracking and analysing the behaviour of summons for advertising purposes.

evidence_quotes.quote: advertising profile of each consumer

paragraphs.text: Thanks to these sophisticated and ubiquitous tracking technologies, such as the real-time bidding system (RTB), personal data sprouts are able to create an accurate advertising profile of each consumer, setting out details ranging from their consumption habits to their financial situation or mental health.

evidence_quotes.quote: This practice of exploiting psychological vulnerabilities leads to constant overstimulation of consumers, pushing them to buy products they may not even need.
algorithms carefully analyse navigation behaviour, preferences and shopping histories to create profiled ads,

paragraphs.text: For example, algorithms carefully analyse navigation behaviour, preferences and shopping histories to create profiled ads, which are likely to induce consumers to succumb to impulsive purchases. This practice of exploiting psychological vulnerabilities leads to constant overstimulation of consumers, pushing them to buy products they may not even need. Targeted online advertising thus proves to be a factor in unreasonable consumption. In addition, there is a concrete risk of data retrenchment and cybercriminals on consumers' privacy, compounded by the constant flow of nil persondata between thousands of businesses.

evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns
tracking and monetising their online behaviour

paragraphs.text: Faced with these abuses, 84 % of consumers are not in favour of tracking and monetising their online behaviour. However, they use deceptive practices and Agree sives, such as dark patterns, to obtain their consent.

evidence_quotes.quote: While consumers theoretically have the right to request the deletion of their information, this is in fact a mirror.

they are forced to remember all the websites they have consulted, search for fidentiability policies to find the list of third parties with which their data has been shared, go one by one to their sites (mostly in English) and collect the email addresses of the people to contact.
it is virtually impossible to delete its data.

paragraphs.text: While consumers theoretically have the right to request the deletion of their information, this is in fact a mirror. On the one hand, they are forced to remember all the websites they have consulted, search for fidentiability policies to find the list of third parties with which their data has been shared, go one by one to their sites (mostly in English) and collect the email addresses of the people to contact. On the other hand, even once the third parties have been identified, UFC-Que Choisir has shown that it is virtually impossible to delete its data.

evidence_quotes.quote: do not offer any means of contact or simply ignore the requests addressed to them.
Only 7 % provide adequate access to personaldonations from a consumer.

paragraphs.text: Indeed, out of more than 1 000 advertising companies examined, more than half (54 %) do not offer any means of contact or simply ignore the requests addressed to them. Only 7 % provide adequate access to personaldonations from a consumer.

evidence_quotes.quote: surveillance advertising
genuine control over their personal data

paragraphs.text: Faced with these systemic abuses, UFC-Que Choisir calls for the tax ruling to strictly regulate or even prohibit surveillance advertising in order to guarantee consumers genuine control over their personal data.
Our study on online advertising: Personal data – The summonstrapped in the Big Brother

evidence_quotes.quote: opinions and ratings left by other users

paragraphs.text: It is often difficult for consumers to assess the actual quality of products and services offered online. Therefore, they attach great importance to opinions and ratings left by other users. According to a UFC-Que Choisir survey, 94 % of respondents are disappointed to pay attention to online opinions, and 73 % believe that they directly influencetheir purchase decay.

evidence_quotes.quote: false opinions.
delete or modify gative opinionsin order to maintain an artificially siltiveimage.

paragraphs.text: However, one major problem persists: false opinions. Many comments are copiedby people who have never tested the produit, or even by fictitious authors. Onapatented platform name, it is sufficient to have a simple email address to publish a notice, without any verification of the authenticity of the purchasing experience. There are insufficient monitoring mechanisms, and some sites do not hesitate to delete or modify gative opinionsin order to maintain an artificially siltiveimage.

evidence_quotes.quote: paid for massively publishing positive mentalcom from a variety of profiles

paragraphs.text: Worse still, some companies rely on providers specialising in the production of fake notices, paid for massively publishing positive mentalcom from a variety of profiles. The rise of artificial intelligence exacerbates this pheno,leading to: automated tools can now generate hundreds of false opinions in a few places, overcrowding the digital space at the risk of making authentic opinions almost invisible.

evidence_quotes.quote: actively encourage the publication of positive opinions
in exchange for benefits such as discounts, repayments or vouchers.

paragraphs.text: Another misleading practice is to actively encourage the publication of positive opinions, while curtailing or removing negative opinions. Summonsare sometimes encouraged to write favourably evacuations in exchange for benefits such as discounts, repayments or vouchers.

evidence_quotes.quote: These practices distort the overall perception of the quality of a product
mislead buyers

paragraphs.text: These practices distort the overall perception of the quality of a product and mislead buyers, who may find themselves acquiring items of poor quality that they would have avoided if the notices had been authentic. This leads to direct economic losses for consumers.

evidence_quotes.quote: prohibited the purchase of false notices and introduced an obligation to check their authenticity when traders claim that they come from genuine consumers.

paragraphs.text: In order to deal with these abuses, the 'Modernisation' Directive prohibited the purchase of false notices and introduced an obligation to check their authenticity when traders claim that they come from genuine consumers. However, despite the entry into force of these rules in
2022, the DGCCRF's checks in 2023 and 2024 revealed that 30 % of the establishments examined were in breach.

evidence_quotes.quote: Traders should behappy to allow the publication of notices only to verified listeners, i.e. those with an authenticated account, purchase history or real interaction with the seller.

paragraphs.text: In the light of these findings, the existing measures need to be strengthened. Traders should behappy to allow the publication of notices only to verified listeners, i.e. those with an authenticated account, purchase history or real interaction with the seller.

evidence_quotes.quote: only verified users (with an account, purchase history and/or actual interaction with the seller) are allowed to publish notices online

paragraphs.text: UFC-Que Choisir requests as well as, in the context of the EPR, and in line with the provisions of the Modernisation Directive, only verified users (with an account, purchase history and/or actual interaction with the seller) are allowed to publish notices online, in order to ensure the reliability and transparency of the assessments available to consumers.

The Digital Fairness Act: UFC-Que positions choose | 14

evidence_quotes.quote: The practice of personalised prices consists of adjusting the tariff of a product or service to the maximum amount that a consumer would be willing to pay

paragraphs.text: The practice of personalised prices consists of adjusting the tariff of a product or service to the maximum amount that a consumer would be willing to pay, determined on the basis of an analysis of his or her personal data. Thus, for the same good or service, two individuals may be offered different prices without being informed.

evidence_quotes.quote: price differentiation
The trader can then adjust the price automatically, without any possibility of negotiation and without explaining the criteria used.

paragraphs.text: While price differentiation is not new, algorithmic personalisation profoundly undermines the traditional business logic. In physical commerce, the seller ignores most of the information about his client and trading takes place in a transparent manner. Conversely, in the digital world, businesses have sophisticated tools that can analyse a wealth of data – historical shopping, online shopping, location, social media interactions, and daily activities – in order to accurately estimate each consumer's willingness to pay. The trader can then adjust the price automatically, without any possibility of negotiation and without explaining the criteria used.

evidence_quotes.quote: the price becomes an opaque and in individualised variable.
leading to discrimination or tariff injustices.

paragraphs.text: These practices greatly accentuate the asymmetry in training between consumers and between catches. They make it impossible to evaluate objectively the real value of the goods and services, as the price becomes an opaque and in individualised variable. Moreover, the data used arenot inaccurate, outdated or interpreted from my biased nature, leading to discrimination or tariff injustices.

evidence_quotes.quote: There are multiple damages caused by price personalisation.
massive collection and use of personal data

identify situations of individual vulnerability – such as a medical emergency, a necessary movement, a pressing need for a service or good – and to pull them on a profit basis by imposing higher tariffs on those who cannot afford to wait for or look for an alternative.
economic discrimination excluding individuals from access to certain goods,
loss of transparency on price formation, preventing comparison between offers.
imposing higher tariffs on those who cannot afford to wait for or look for an alternative.

paragraphs.text: There are multiple damages caused by price personalisation. It can lead to massive collection and use of personal data, economic discrimination excluding individuals from access to certain goods, and a loss of transparency on price formation, preventing comparison between offers. On the other hand, they make it possible to identify situations of individual vulnerability – such as a medical emergency, a necessary movement, a pressing need for a service or good – and to pull them on a profit basis by imposing higher tariffs on those who cannot afford to wait for or look for an alternative.
European law, as it stands, provides only very limited

evidence_quotes.quote: partial information requirements, which are often insufficient to ensure that they are fully transparent.

paragraphs.text: protection against such practices, merely requiring partial information requirements, which are often insufficient to ensure that they are fully transparent.

evidence_quotes.quote: pricing technique based on the use of personal data with the aim of adjusting pricesto melt individual behavioural predictions

paragraphs.text: In this context, UFC-Que Choisir requests that the tax ruling explicitly prohibits any pricing technique based on the use of personal data with the aim of adjusting pricesto melt individual behavioural predictions.

evidence_quotes.quote: personalised or collective discounts that are not based on profiling

paragraphs.text: However, that prohibition should not cover personalised or collective discounts that are not based on profiling, such as programs of loyalty or transparent geographic offers.

evidence_quotes.quote: opacity of price-fixing mechanisms

paragraphs.text: Finally, in view of the opacity of price-fixing mechanisms, the burden of proof of compliance with legal obligations must lie with the persons concerned. They must be able to demonstrate, in a clear and verifiable manner, that their tariff supporters comply with the principles of transpa ference, fairness and non-discrimination.

evidence_quotes.quote: In the attention economy, ric interfaces are often designed to retain users for as long as possible, in order to maximise datacollection, consumption expenditure and advertising revenues.
infinitedropping
Some applications even mask the phone clock
lubrication (orgamification)

paragraphs.text: In the attention economy, ric interfaces are often designed to retain users for as long as possible, in order to maximise datacollection, consumption expenditure and advertising revenues. The most common techniques for maintaining consumer management include infinitedropping, automatic reading of videos, multiplication of notifications, lubrication (orgamification), ephemeral content, recom exemptions for continuous use or penalties for inactivity. Some applications even mask the phone clock, making it difficult for users to track the time spent online (a technique also practised in casinos).

evidence_quotes.quote: Research shows that these design mechanisms can induce behaviours similar to those observed in addictions.

paragraphs.text: Research shows that these design mechanisms can induce behaviours similar to those observed in addictions. This is due to the role of dopamine, a neurotransmitter associated with feelings of pleasure, satisfaction and excitation. This substance is formed not only in pleasant experiences such as sport or social interactions, but also in anticipation of a reward. For example, every new content discovered by challenging one page, every video that launches itself automatically, each notification received or each visual arena prompting interaction triggers a small dopamine discharge.

evidence_quotes.quote: The algorithms, able to analyse divided behaviours, then adapt content and stimulate them to strengthen this addictive effect.

paragraphs.text: Over time, this repeated stimulation may create an increasing need to repeat action to regain the same sense of pleasure. The algorithms, able to analyse divided behaviours, then adapt content and stimulate them to strengthen this addictive effect. This circle can lead to almost repetitive consultation of the screens, sometimes several hundred times a day, pending further immediate satisfaction.

evidence_quotes.quote: addictive design goes far beyond social media.

paragraphs.text: Today, addictive design goes far beyond social media. It extends to e-commerce sites, video games, streaming platforms and many other ricalsservices. Developers themselves claim their ability to mobilise users' cognitive and emotional springs to retain them.

evidence_quotes.quote: addictive

paragraphs.text: While capturing the consumer's attention is part of marketing and is not in itself an unfair tick, addictive

evidence_quotes.quote: this addictive design
compulsive features (such as impulsive purchases)

paragraphs.text: design has worrying effects. It can cause psy chial-disorders (anxiety, depression), compulsive features (such as impulsive purchases), as well as physical consequences (sleep deprivation, sedentary or even altering cognitive functions). Children and adolescents, whose brain is still developing, are particularly vulnerable due to their low self-monitoring capacity. Combined with online purchases, this addictive design also favours unintended exemptions and economic losses.

evidence_quotes.quote: deactivate by default all native functions that may induce addictive behaviour

paragraphs.text: In response to these findings, UFC-Que Choisir requests that the EPR requires online service providers to deactivate by default all native functions that may induce addictive behaviour, such as infinite scrolling, automatic reading, overload of notices, ludification, ephemeral content, recom exemptions for prolonged use or criminals in the event of inactivity. For minors, the use of these addictive design functionalities should be strictly prohibited.

The Digital Fairness Act: UFC-Que positions choose | 16

According to figures from the French government, 96 % of young people use the internet on a daily basis. This public, particularly exposed to naked risks in the Americas, must be protected. A number of major ticketing problems are now threatening the well-being of minors online:

evidence_quotes.quote: Addictive design

paragraphs.text: Addictive design: platforms such as TikTok or Instagram exploit youngpeople's attitudes, encouraging them to spend considerable time online and exposing them to harmful content.
Influence marketing: 36 % of children aged 6-12 do not perceive the clothing published by influencers as advertising.

evidence_quotes.quote: Integrated purchases: 64 % of children with children think each month between EUR 1 and EUR 20 in video games, a significant amount for their age.

paragraphs.text: Integrated purchases: 64 % of children with children think each month between EUR 1 and EUR 20 in video games, a significant amount for their age.

evidence_quotes.quote: targeted advertising based on the collection of their data

paragraphs.text: The exploitation of personal data: children are particularly popular in the face of targeted advertising based on the collection of their data.

evidence_quotes.quote: online age verification remains largely ineffective.

paragraphs.text: In France, a digital majority was introduced in 2023: it now takes 15 years to create an account on a social network alone. However, online age verification remains largely ineffective.

evidence_quotes.quote: they say that they check the age of users, but in reality this is more a moral decoration than a strict check.
enter a date of birth, which is not checked.

paragraphs.text: In order to access certain sites that are prohibited for minors, such as alcohol sales sites, the persons concerned only have to enter a date of birth, which is not checked. It is therefore simply possible to enter a false one. For example, with a website name, they say that they check the age of users, but in reality this is more a moral decoration than a strict check.

evidence_quotes.quote: recorded the exact address of the content accessed
shared the users' IP address with Amazon's hosting services

paragraphs.text: On the other hand, more sophisticated age verification systems systematically raise data protection concerns. Indeed, the technical analysis of an age verification tool put in place in France by certain phic pornographic sites, following the introduction of a new legal obligation, showed that it recorded the exact address of the content accessed and shared the users' IP address with Amazon's hosting services.

evidence_quotes.quote: circumvented through VPN

paragraphs.text: In addition, even the most complex technical systems can be easily circumvented through VPN, accessible to all, including minors. Furthermore, this use of VPN is lagging behind new risks for consumers.

evidence_quotes.quote: Effective protection of minors depends primarily on comprehensive regulation of online practices.

paragraphs.text: Effective protection of minors depends primarily on comprehensive regulation of online practices. Strengthening the security and ethics of all digital services protects not only young people, but also all-summers. However, special attention must be paid to children because of their increased likelihood.

evidence_quotes.quote: designed from their inception as safe and fair spaces.

paragraphs.text: Therefore, UFC-Que Choisir calls for the EPR to continue and strengthen the protection of minors from digital hazards. All sites and replications must be designed from their inception as safe and fair spaces. Bygarlic, UFC-Que Choisir calls for any age verification mechanisms to protect privacy and not involve the use of the personal data ofsumers.
Our European position: Better safe than sorry – How to keep children safe online in the EU

The Digital Fairness Act: UFC-Que positions choose | 17

evidence_quotes.quote: Some obvious deceptive practices can be easily disguised with screenshots

paragraphs.text: One of the main obstacles to the scrupulous application of consumer law to unfair online commercial practices is the burden of proof. Some obvious deceptive practices can be easily disguised with screenshots, but in my jority, consumers have little access to the information needed to understand, let alone prove, the mechanisms used by traders.

evidence_quotes.quote: artificially inflated demand for a product to create a false sense of urgency
customised according to opaque criteria

paragraphs.text: How could a consumer know, for example, that a seller has artificially inflated demand for a product to create a false sense of urgency, or that an offer has been customised according to opaque criteria to influence their purchasing decision? The information asymmetry here is total: consumers have no visibility on algorithms, personalisation strategies or data used. It is therefore possible for them to prove that these practices are unfair.

evidence_quotes.quote: manipulation is often based on processes that aren't acceptable to consumers.

paragraphs.text: Currently, the Unfair Commercial Practices Directive (UCPD) provides for a reduction of the burden of proof only in the case of misleading factual assessments, such as when a trader attributes to a product specific-mild health effects without scientific basis. On the other hand, this provision does not apply to other types of unfair practices, in particular those specific to the digital environment, where manipulation is often based on processes that aren't acceptable to consumers.

In such situations of asymmetry of information, where undertakings alone possess the evidence per se to prove or refute afair practice, it would be unfair to place the burden of proof on the consumer. The EPR should therefore amend the UCPD Directive in order to
introducestructural rebalancing and to alleviate or even reverse this burden.

evidence_quotes.quote: where a commercial practice is based on mechanisms the operation of which is not accessible to the consumer

paragraphs.text: These adjustments should be based on a number of principles. First, where a commercial practice is based on mechanisms the operation of which is not accessible to the consumer, it should be for the trader to provide a clear, detailed and relevant explanation of how that practice operates and to demonstrate that it does not constitute an unfair tick. In the absence of sufficient, accurate and verifiable incitation, the meadow should be presumed to be unfair.

Secondly, a presumption of unfairness should apply where a practice has already been found to be unfair by a final decision of abumper sorting or a national consumer protection authority or by a common position of the European Network of Consumer Protection Authorities (CPC Network).

Finally, those presumptions should also extend to actions for damages brought on the basis of Article 11a of the UCPD Directive, in order to ensure a real possibility of compensation for injured consumers.

evidence_quotes.quote: algorithmic opacity

paragraphs.text: Ultimately, the EPR should enshrine a principle of procedural fairness: in a native environment characterised by strong informational asymmetry and algorithmic opacity, it is up to traders – not consumers – to demonstrate fairness and transparency in their commercial practices.
Our European position: EU consumer protection 2.0 – Protecting fairness and consumer choice in a digital economy
UFCQ

Founded in 1951, UFC-Que Choisir is the main French consumer association. It informs, advises, defends consumers and engages in responsible consumption, respectful of health issues, mental surroundings and society.
